name: Scandinavian Languages
description: Distinguish between Norwegian (Bokmål), Swedish, and Danish.

questions.explanation: Norwegian uses 'jeg' (like Danish) and 'eple' with a 'p'. It also uses the 'ø' and 'æ' characters.
Danish uses 'æble' with a 'b', whereas Norwegian uses a 'p'.
Swedish is easily identified by the use of 'ä' and 'ö' instead of 'æ' and 'ø'.
The 'hv-' start for question words and 'd' in 'hedder' are characteristic of Danish.
Norwegian drops the 'd' in 'hva' and uses 't' in 'heter' where Danish uses 'd'.
Swedish uses 'Vad' and 'heter' (with a 't') along with its distinct vowel markings.
Uses 'hvor' (where) and the compound 'togstasjonen' with a 'g'.
Danish uses 'stationen' (with a 't') rather than the Norwegian 'stasjonen'.
Swedish uses 'Var' for 'Where' and the 'å' character in 'tåg'.
The word 'flicka' (girl) and 'och' (and) are uniquely Swedish in this group.
Norwegian uses 'gutt' (boy) and 'jente' (girl), and 'og' for 'and'.
Danish uses 'dreng' (boy) and 'pige' (girl).
Uses 'vår' (our) and 'butikk' with a double 'k'.
Swedish uses 'Välkommen' with an 'ä' and a single 'k' in 'butik'.
Danish uses 'vores' for 'our' and a single 'k' in 'butik'.
The 'ö' and 'ä' characters are the primary giveaway for Swedish.
Uses 'ø' and the verb 'gjør' (makes/does).
Danish uses 'gør', which is spelled with a 'ø' but lacks the 'j' found in Norwegian 'gjør'.
Swedish uses 'många' (many) and 'ä' in 'bäckar'.
Danish uses 'mange' and 'bække'.
Norwegian uses 'mange' and 'bekker' with a 'k' where Danish uses 'k'.
Swedish uses 'kärleken' (the love) with an 'ä'.
Norwegian uses 'kjærlighet' with a 'j' and 'æ'.
Danish uses 'kærlighed' with a 'd' ending where Norwegian uses 't'.

questions.text: Det er deilig å være norsk.
questions.explanation: Though this is a famous slogan about Norway, 'deilig' and the syntax are classically Danish.

questions.text: Det er deilig å være norsk.
questions.explanation: In written form (Bokmål), this sentence is identical to Danish, but often associated with Norwegian tourism.

questions.text: Det är härligt att vara svensk.
questions.explanation: Uses 'är' instead of 'er' and 'att' instead of 'å'.
Uses 'viktigste' (most important) and 'frokost' (breakfast).
Danish uses 'morgenmad' for breakfast, whereas in Norwegian 'frokost' is breakfast.
Swedish uses 'frukost' (u instead of o) and 'är'.
